---
title: "Final Project"
author: "Santiago D. Barber"
date: "February 3rd 2026"
output:
  html_document:
    theme:
      bootswatch: darkly
    toc: true
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
---

```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(echo = TRUE, eval=FALSE, message=FALSE, warning=FALSE)
```

```{r libraries, eval=TRUE}
library(ggplot2)
library(DESeq2)
library(magrittr)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(readr)
library(ggrepel)
library(tibble)
library(biomaRt)
library(readr)
library(purrr)
```
# Introduction

This project aims to analyze the "BATLAS" dataset and quantify the genome expression profile of brown adipose tissue (BAT). The purpose of this quantification is to incorporate BAT transcript abundance levels to the GTEx consortium dataset. GTEx is a large-scale project that has generated a comprehensive resource of gene expression and regulation across multiple human tissues. Despite the importance of BAT in human metabolism and energy homeostasis, GTEx does not include any samples from this tissue. By integrating the BAT data with GTEx, we can gain insights into the unique gene expression patterns of BAT and how they compare to other tissues in the human body. This integration will allow us to better understand the molecular mechanisms underlying BAT function and its potential role in metabolic diseases.

As part of the integration, I 

#### Original Publication

Perdikari A, Leparc GG, Balaz M, Pires ND, Lidell ME, Sun W, Fernandez-Albert F, Müller S, Akchiche N, Dong H, Balazova L, Opitz L, Röder E, Klein H, Stefanicka P, Varga L, Nuutila P, Virtanen KA, Niemi T, Taittonen M, Rudofsky G, Ukropec J, Enerbäck S, Stupka E, Neubauer H, Wolfrum C. **BATLAS: Deconvoluting Brown Adipose Tissue.** *Cell Reports.* 2018 Oct 16;25(3):784–797.e4

#### Who generated the data

The BATLAS RNA-seq data (ENA study ERP022805) were generated by the BATLAS study team (referenced above). The main contributor was Christian Wolfrum’s group at the Institute of Food, Nutrition and Health, ETH Zürich.

Deep neck (brown adipose tissue, BAT) and adjacent white subcutaneous adipose tissue (WAT) samples were obtained from the lower third of the neck by an experienced ENT surgeon from 18 individuals (1 Male/ 17 Female) during neck surgery under general anesthesia, age between 22 to 73 years old and BMI between 19 to 34 kg/m2. The deep neck adipose tissue sample was taken from pre- and paravertebral space between the common carotid and trachea in case of thyroid surgery and just laterally to the carotid sheath in case of branchial cleft cyst surgery. 

In all cases, the surgical approach was sufficient to reach and sample the deep neck adipose tissue without any additional morbidity. Patients with malignant disease and subjects younger than 18 years were excluded from participation in the study. Adipose tissue samples were immediately cleaned from blood and connective tissue, and frozen in liquid nitrogen until further processing. The clinical study on obese subjects (20/42 Male/Female; age 40.5 ± 1.6 years; BMI 41.2 ± 0.9 kg/m2) undergoing caloric restriction with a daily intake of 800 kcal was approved by the ethics committee at the Uni- versity Hospital of Heidelberg, Germany (S-365/2007). It conforms to the ethical guidelines of the 2000 Helsinki declaration. All participants provided witnessed written informed consent prior to entering the study. The study was registered as NCT00773565.

#### How was the RNA extracted?

Total RNA was extracted from whole adipose tissues using Trizol reagent (Invitrogen) according to the manufacturer’s instructions.

#### What library prep was used?

Deep neck `BAT` and subcutaneous white adipose tissue `WAT` RNA-Seq libraries were prepared using 25 ng of total RNA input with the TrueSeq RNA Sample Prep Kit v2 (Illumina) producing an average 275 bp fragment including adapters. 

#### How was mRNA enriched?

According to the TrueSeq RNA Sample Prep Kit v2 (Illumina) user manual, the messenger RNA is first purified using polyA selection, then chemically fragmented and converted into single-stranded cDNA using random hexamer priming.

#### What cell type or tissue was used?

Brown adipose tissue (BAT) and white adipose tissue (WAT) from human deep neck surgeries

#### What was the treatment/experimental condition?

The "experimental condition" would be Brown adipose tissue (BAT). I want to know if the BAT samples are indeed enriched in brown adipose tissue markers compared to the WAT samples, and if the differentially expressed genes betweem both tissues supports the hypothesis that these samples are indeed coming from BAT tissue.

>Hypothesis: BAT samples have been correctly collected from patients and show a gene expression profile consistent with that of brown adipose tissue, including the expression of brown adipocyte-specific genes.

#### What sequencing platform was used?

In the final step before RNA-Seq, eight individual libraries were normalized and pooled together using the adaptor indices supplied by the manufacturer. RNA-Seq was performed as 50 bp, single reads and 7 bases index read on an Illumina HiSeq2000 instrument. Approximately 20–30 million reads per sample were obtained.

#### Citations

Orava, J., Nuutila, P., Lidell, M.E., Oikonen, V., Noponen, T., Viljanen, T., Scheinin, M., Taittonen, M., Niemi, T., Enerba ¨ ck, S., and Virtanen, K.A. (2011). Different metabolic responses of human brown adipose tissue to activation by cold and insulin. Cell Metab. 14, 272–279.

# Methods

## Downloading & documenting the data

The BATLAS dataset has been fully uploaded to ENA with accession number `ERP022805`. Further information about the data can be found [here](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/browser/view/ERP022805). 

The following command was used to download the manifest of the study

```{bash }
wget -O ERP022805_read_run.tsv 'https://www.ebi.ac.uk/ena/portal/api/filereport?accession=ERP022805&result=read_run&fields=study_accession,sample_accession,secondary_sample_accession,experiment_accession,run_accession,scientific_name,experiment_title,fastq_ftp,sample_alias&format=tsv&download=true&limit=0'
```

The BATLAS dataset is composed of two different cohorst. `Cohort1` are patients those from which a PET scan was performed before surgery, and `Cohort2` are patients those from which a PET scan was NOT performed before surgery. 

This analysis focuses on`Cohort2`, in which samples are expected to be more heterogeneous giving the missing PET scan. We can use a `while` loop to download all of the samples that have a `sample_alias` that starts with `E387`, since those are the ones belonguing to `Cohort2`. To do this, we can pipe the output of the `awk` function (the ftp downlad links at column `$8`) into a `while` loop that uses `read` to store the download links into a variable `path`. Then, for each `path`, build the dowlonad url adding "ftp://" and run `wget`:

```{bash}
awk -F'\t' 'NR>1 && $9 ~ /^E387/ {print $8}' ERP022805_read_run.tsv |
while read -r path; do
  url="ftp://$path"
  echo "======= Starting download of $url"
  wget -c "$url"
done
```

For convenience, a `sample_table.tsv` file was generated that contains all useful information needed for downstream analyses:

```{bash}
awk -F'\t' '
BEGIN{OFS="\t"}
NR==1{ for (i=1;i<=NF;i++) {
    if ($i=="sample_alias") sa=i
    if ($i=="run_accession") ra=i}
  print "run_accession","sample_alias","sample_number","fat_type" 
  next
}
sa && ra && $sa ~ /^E387_/{
  split($sa,a,"_")
  fat=a[3] "_" a[4]
  print $ra, $sa, a[2], fat}' \
  ERP022805_read_run.tsv > sample_table.tsv
```

## Align the FASTQ file with an appropriate aligner

### Alignment

Since the final objective of this project is to merge the resulting transcript abundances with the GTEx data, I will use the same genome build and GENECODE version that [GTEx](https://www.gtexportal.org/home/releaseInfoPage) uses.

To download the genome annotation and FASTA:

```{bash}
wget https://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/gencode/Gencode_human/release_39/gencode.v39.annotation.gtf.gz
gunzip -c gencode.v39.annotation.gtf.gz > gencode.v39.annotation.gtf

wget https://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/gencode/Gencode_human/release_39/GRCh38.primary_assembly.genome.fa.gz
gunzip GRCh38.primary_assembly.genome.fa.gz
``` 

To create the STAR index and align the samples, we can use the following script and `sbatch`. GTEx uses STAR version `2.7.10a` while our `angsd` environment uses `2.7.11a`. Ideally, we would like to use the same version to maximize cross-dataset comparability; but I will be using this environment to keep things more reproducible.

The `STAR_index_and_alignment.sh` file checks if the STAR index already exists. Then, it loops over all `.fastq` files in the `FASTQ_DIR`, aligns them using STAR, and outputs sorted BAM files to the `BAM_DIR`. Lastly, it indexes each BAM file using `samtools`.

For STAR alignment, the `--sjdbOverhang 49` was used so splice junction sequences were optimized for the read length when creating the INDEX. When running the alignment, `outSAMattributes All` was specified to get all of the attributes, and`--outSAMtype BAM SortedByCoordinate` was specified so the outputs would be sorted and `BAM`. Lastly, each `.bam` file was indexed using `samtools index`.

`STAR_index_and_alignment.sh`:
```{bash}
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#SBATCH --job-name=star_hg38_v39
#SBATCH --output=STAR_RUN.out
#SBATCH --error=STAR_RUN.err
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=64G

set -euo pipefail

# User-configurable paths

PROJECT_DIR="/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project"
INDEX_DIR="${PROJECT_DIR}/STAR_index_GENCODE_v39"
FASTQ_DIR="${PROJECT_DIR}/FASTQ"
BAM_DIR="${PROJECT_DIR}/BAMs"

# Fasta and GTF 
GENOME_FA="/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/GRCh38.primary_assembly.genome.fa"
GTF_FILE="/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/gencode.v39.annotation.gtf"

# Since fastq files are 52bp
SJDB_OVERHANG=49

# Environment
source /home/fs01/sdb4002/miniconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh
conda activate angsd

# Build STAR index using downloaded reference and FASTA (GENCODE v39, GRCh38/hg38)
mkdir -p "${INDEX_DIR}"
mkdir -p "${BAM_DIR}"

if [[ -s "${INDEX_DIR}/Genome" && -s "${INDEX_DIR}/SA" && -s "${INDEX_DIR}/SAindex" ]]; then # check if the index already exist...
  echo "STAR index already exists in ${INDEX_DIR}; skipping Index creation."
else
  echo "STAR index not found (or incomplete); generating in ${INDEX_DIR}..."
  STAR \
    --runMode genomeGenerate \
    --runThreadN "${SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK}" \
    --genomeDir "${INDEX_DIR}" \
    --genomeFastaFiles "${GENOME_FA}" \
    --sjdbGTFfile "${GTF_FILE}" \
    --sjdbOverhang "${SJDB_OVERHANG}"

# Align each FASTQ in FASTQ directory and write BAMs to BAM directory
shopt -s nullglob # so it does not crash if FASTQs are not found
FASTQS=("${FASTQ_DIR}"/*.fastq.gz)
(( ${#FASTQS[@]} > 0 )) || { echo "No FASTQs found in ${FASTQ_DIR}" >&2; exit 1; }

for fastq in "${FASTQS[@]}"; do
  sample="$(basename "${fastq}" .fastq.gz)"
  prefix="${BAM_DIR}/${sample}.STAR."

  STAR \
    --runMode alignReads \
    --runThreadN "${SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK:-1}" \
    --genomeDir "${INDEX_DIR}" \
    --readFilesIn "${fastq}" \
    --readFilesCommand zcat \
    --outFileNamePrefix "${prefix}" \
    --outSAMattributes All \
    --outSAMtype BAM SortedByCoordinate

  samtools index "${prefix}Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam"
done
```

### FastQC

FastQC and MultiQC were run on an interactive session on the terminal.  

```{bash}
mkdir FASTQC_BAM && mkdir MULTIQC_BAM

BAMS=(/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/BAMs/*.bam)

for bam in "${BAMS[@]}"; do
  fastqc -o "/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/FASTQC_BAM" "${bam}"
done
```
```{bash}
conda activate multiqc
multiqc "/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/FASTQC_BAM" \
  -o "/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/MULTIQC_BAM" \
  -n multiqc_bam_fastqc_report
```

FASTQC shows samples fail `Per base sequence content` but there are no overrepresented sequences or adapter contamination. This could be caused by random hexamer priming bias (the “random” primers aren’t perfectly random).

The analysis also fails `Sequence duplication levels`, with only around 20% of the reads remaining if the samples were to be deduplicated. Since FASTQ cannot tell PCR duplicates from “real” duplicates of abundant transcripts because it looks at raw sequences only.

MultiQC shows samples pass all other metrics:
```{R}
knitr::include_graphics("MultiQC.png")
```

Overrepresented sequences were explored to have an idea of what is driving these sequence duplication observations. As an example, I decided to chechk one of the `.fastq` files arbitrarily

```{bash}
zcat ERR2225804.fastq.gz | awk 'NR%4==2' | sort | uniq -c | sort -nr | head -20
```
```{}
  10819 CTTGGATTAAGGCGACAGCGATTTCTAGGATAGTCAGTAGAATTAGAATTGT
   8956 CCCGTATCGAAGGCCTTTTTGGACAGGTGGTGTGTGGTGGCCTTGGTATGTG
   8925 CCGTCATCTACTCTACCATCTTTGCAGGCACACTCATCACAGCGCTAAGCTC
   7545 CAAGACGCTACTTCCCCTATCATAGAAGAGCTTATCACCTTTCATGATCACG
   7373 CCAAAATGAACGAAAATCTGTTCGCTTCATTCATTGCCCCCACAATCCTAGG
   7019 CCCAAACCCACTCCACCTTACTACCAGACAACCTTAGCCAAACCATTTACCC
   6737 CTAGCATTTACCATCTCACTTCTAGGAATACTAGTATATCGCTCACACCTCA
   6417 CACATGCCTATCATATAGTAAAACCCAGCCCATGACCCCTAACAGGGGCCCT
   6337 GTTTGGTCTAGGGTGTAGCCTGAGAATAGGGGAAATCAGTGAATGAAGCCTC
   6213 GTCGTGTAGTACGATGTCTAGTGATGAGTTTGCTAATACAATGCCAGTCAGG
   6189 CTCAAATCATGAAAATTATTAATATTACTGCTGTTAGAGAAATGAATGAGCC
   6039 CCAAAATCCATTTCACTATCATATTCATCGGCGTAAATCTAACTTTCTTCCC
   5814 GTGGAAGTGAGCTACAACGTAGTACGTGTCGTGTAGTACGATGTCTAGTGAT
   5582 CTAAATACTACCGTATGGCCCACCATAATTACCCCCATACTCCTTACACTAT
   5263 GTAAAACCCAGCCCATGACCCCTAACAGGGGCCCTCTCAGCCCTCCTAATGA
   5161 CTGAGATGTTAGTATTAGTTAGTTTTGTTGTGAGTGTTAGGAAAAGGGCATA
   5101 GTTTGATTAGTCATTGTTGGGTGGTGATTAGTCGGTTGTTGATGAGATATTT
   5032 CTGGCATTGTATTAGCAAACTCATCACTAGACATCGTACTACACGACACGTA
   5017 CTGCTGTTAGAGAAATGAATGAGCCTACAGATGATAGGATGTTTCATGTGGT
   5016 AAATGAATGAGCCTACAGATGATAGGATGTTTCATGTGGTGTATGCATCGGG
```

Upon conducting a BLAST search, most of these highly duplicated sequences come from mitochondrial genes. BAT is a tissue highly enriched in mitocondria, so it is no surprising to find this type of enrichment. However, WAT should not be as enriched. To check this, we will check the percentage of reads aligning to the mitochondrion `chrM`:

```{bash}
for bam in *.bam; do
  printf "%s\t" "$bam"
  samtools idxstats "$bam" | awk 'BEGIN{mt=0; tot=0}
         {tot += $3}
         ($1=="MT" || $1=="chrM") {mt = $3}
         END {
           if (tot==0) {printf("MT reads: %d / %d (NA)\n", mt, tot); exit}
           printf("MT reads: %d / %d (%.2f%%)\n", mt, tot, 100*mt/tot)
         }'
done
```

```{}
ERR2225804.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 7076507 / 32789062 (21.58%)
ERR2225805.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 4870472 / 28969581 (16.81%)
ERR2225806.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 4038709 / 32059933 (12.60%)
ERR2225807.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 4838939 / 32202031 (15.03%)
ERR2225808.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 10036141 / 30709186 (32.68%)
ERR2225809.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 4447025 / 36458272 (12.20%)
ERR2225810.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 7328692 / 32043409 (22.87%)
ERR2225811.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 4280930 / 31122168 (13.76%)
ERR2225812.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 16945371 / 31865569 (53.18%)
ERR2225813.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 5330467 / 33934060 (15.71%)
ERR2225814.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 8937631 / 30847538 (28.97%)
ERR2225815.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 5046633 / 28589238 (17.65%)
ERR2225816.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 11707747 / 37042422 (31.61%)
ERR2225817.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 5155179 / 30675231 (16.81%)
ERR2225818.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 8408498 / 28732392 (29.26%)
ERR2225819.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 2909748 / 30491456 (9.54%)
ERR2225820.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 7829343 / 32235658 (24.29%)
ERR2225821.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 4269606 / 31373385 (13.61%)
ERR2225822.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam   MT reads: 4777305 / 30491358 (15.67%)
```

We can calculate the average percentage of reads that map to `chrM` across the two different sample groups that we are studying:

*BAT (brownv fat, n=10): 27.27%*

*WAT (white fat, n=10): 14.08%*

### Gene-level quantification with featureCounts

After alignment, a raw counts matrix was built for downstream differential expression analysis. `featureCounts`, from the Subread package, was used on the coordinate-sorted BAM files produced by `STAR`. Because these data are single-end, we do not use paired-end options. We count reads overlapping `exon` features and summarize them at the `gene_id` level using the same GENCODE v39 annotation that was used for alignment.

`Feature_counts.sh`:
```{bash}
#!/usr/bin/env bash
#SBATCH --job-name=featurecounts_batlas
#SBATCH --output=/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/featureCounts.out
#SBATCH --error=/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/featureCounts.err
#SBATCH --time=08:00:00
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=32G

set -euo pipefail
shopt -s nullglob

PROJECT_DIR="/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project"
BAM_DIR="${PROJECT_DIR}/BAMs"
COUNTS_DIR="${PROJECT_DIR}/counts"
GTF_FILE="${PROJECT_DIR}/gencode.v39.annotation.gtf"

source /home/fs01/sdb4002/miniconda3/etc/profile.d/conda.sh
conda activate angsd

mkdir -p "${COUNTS_DIR}"

BAMS=("${BAM_DIR}"/*.bam)
(( ${#BAMS[@]} > 0 )) || { echo "No BAM files found in ${BAM_DIR}" >&2; exit 1; }

featureCounts \
  -T "${SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK:-1}" \
  -a "${GTF_FILE}" \
  -o "${COUNTS_DIR}/BATLAS_featureCounts.txt" \
  -t exon \
  -g gene_id \
  "${BAMS[@]}"
```

```{}

        ==========     _____ _    _ ____  _____  ______          _____  
        =====         / ____| |  | |  _ \|  __ \|  ____|   /\   |  __ \ 
          =====      | (___ | |  | | |_) | |__) | |__     /  \  | |  | |
            ====      \___ \| |  | |  _ <|  _  /|  __|   / /\ \ | |  | |
              ====    ____) | |__| | |_) | | \ \| |____ / ____ \| |__| |
        ==========   |_____/ \____/|____/|_|  \_\______/_/    \_\_____/
          v2.0.6

//========================== featureCounts setting ===========================\\
||                                                                            ||
||             Input files : 21 BAM files                                     ||
||                                                                            ||
||                           ERR2225804.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225805.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225806.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225807.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225808.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225809.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225810.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225811.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225812.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225813.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225814.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225815.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225816.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225817.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225818.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225819.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225820.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225821.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225822.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                           ERR2225823.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam    ||
||                                                                            ||
||             Output file : BATLAS_featureCounts.txt                         ||
||                 Summary : BATLAS_featureCounts.txt.summary                 ||
||              Paired-end : no                                               ||
||        Count read pairs : no                                               ||
||              Annotation : gencode.v39.annotation.gtf (GTF)                 ||
||      Dir for temp files : /athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/ ... ||
||                                                                            ||
||                 Threads : 1                                                ||
||                   Level : meta-feature level                               ||
||      Multimapping reads : not counted                                      ||
|| Multi-overlapping reads : not counted                                      ||
||   Min overlapping bases : 1                                                ||
||                                                                            ||
\\============================================================================//

//================================= Running ==================================\\
||                                                                            ||
|| Load annotation file gencode.v39.annotation.gtf ...                        ||
||    Features : 1552754                                                      ||
||    Meta-features : 61533                                                   ||
||    Chromosomes/contigs : 25                                                ||
||                                                                            ||
|| Process BAM file ERR2225804.STAR.Aligned.sortedByCoord.out.bam...          ||
```

`featureCounts` output includes annotation columns followed by one column per BAM file. To generate a cleaner gene-by-sample counts matrix the the sample names where changed to the `sample_alias` from our `sample_table.tsv`.

```{bash}
awk 'BEGIN{FS=OFS="\t"}
FNR==NR {
  if (FNR > 1) alias[$1] = $2 # store sample_alias by run_accession from sample_table
  next
}
/^#/ {next}
$1 == "Geneid" && !seen_header {
  printf "Geneid"
  for (i=7; i<=NF; i++) {
    sample = $i # get the sample name from the header
    sub(/^.*\//, "", sample) # strip path
    sub(/\.STAR\.Aligned\.sortedByCoord\.out\.bam$/, "", sample) # strip STAR suffix
    printf OFS alias[sample] # replace with sample_alias
  }
  printf "\n"
  seen_header = 1
  next
}
$1 != "Geneid" { # for data rows, keep geneid and counts
  printf $1 # "Geneid"
  for (i=7; i<=NF; i++) printf OFS $i # 
  printf "\n"
}' /athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/sample_table.tsv \
   /athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/counts/BATLAS_featureCounts.txt \
   > /athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/counts/BATLAS_counts_matrix.tsv
```

The resulting `BATLAS_counts_matrix.tsv` file contains raw gene counts, with genes in rows and samples in columns, and can be used directly as input for tools such as `DESeq2` or `edgeR`.

### RSeQC for RNA-seq QC on BAM files

The `read_distribution.py` from the RSeQC package was used on an aligned and sorted BAM file to check what genomic features reads are mapping to. 

To run RSeQC, a `.bed` file is required. To generate this file, the GTF annotation was first converted to `genePred` format using `gtfToGenePred`, and then converted to `BED` format using `genePredToBed`. Both of these tools are available in the `ucsc-tools` package on bioconda.

```{bash}
conda create -y -n RSeQC \
  python=3.10 \
  rseqc \
  samtools \
  ucsc-gtftogenepred \
  ucsc-genepredtobed \
  -c conda-forge -c bioconda
```

```{bash}
conda activate RSeQC

gtfToGenePred gencode.v39.annotation.gtf gencode.v39.annotation.genePred
genePredToBed gencode.v39.annotation.genePred gencode.v39.annotation.bed
```

```{bash}
BAMS="/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/BAMs"
BED="/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/gencode.v39.annotation.bed"
OUT_DIR="/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/RSeQC_read_distribution"

for bam in "$BAMS"/*.bam; do
  sample=$(basename "$bam" .bam)
  read_distribution.py -i "$bam" -r "$BED" > "$OUT_DIR/${sample}.read_distribution.txt"
done
```

RSeQC results were parsed to extract the percentage of reads mapping to different genomic features for each sample and compiled into a single `read_distribution_summary.tsv` file.

```{bash}
OUT_DIR="/athena/angsd/scratch/sdb4002/Final_project/RSeQC_read_distribution"

{
  printf "sample\tgroup\tpercent\n"
  for file in "$OUT_DIR"/*.read_distribution.txt; do
    sample=$(basename "$file" .read_distribution.txt)
    awk -v s="$sample" '
      $1 == "Total" && $2 == "Assigned" && $3 == "Tags" { # store the total number of assigned reads for this sample
        assigned = $4 # the number of reads assigned to any feature
      }
      $1 ~ /^(CDS_Exons|5'\''UTR_Exons|3'\''UTR_Exons|Introns|TSS_up_10kb|TES_down_10kb)$/ && assigned > 0 {
        percent = ($3 / assigned) * 100 # calculate percentage of reads mapping to this feature
        printf "%s\t%s\t%.2f\n", s, $1, percent
      }
    ' "$file"
  done
} > "$OUT_DIR/read_distribution_summary.tsv"
```

```{r, eval=TRUE}
ggplot(read_tsv("read_distribution_summary.tsv"), aes(x = group, y = percent)) +
  geom_boxplot(outlier.shape = NA, fill = "grey85", color = "black") +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.15, size = 2, alpha = 0.8, color = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    x = "Genomic feature",
    y = "Percent of reads",
    title = "RSeQC read distribution across samples") +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))
```

### DESeq2 analysis of BAT vs WAT differential expression

Raw counts data and the sample table generated before were imported into R and used to create a `DESeqDataSet` object. The `condition` variable was created based on the `fat_type` column, and the design formula was set to `~ condition` to compare BAT vs WAT.
```{r, eval=TRUE}
RawCounts <- read.table("BATLAS_counts_matrix.tsv", header = TRUE, sep = "\t", check.names = FALSE)
SampleTable <- read.table("sample_table.tsv", header = TRUE, sep = "\t", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)

row.names(RawCounts) <- make.names(RawCounts$Geneid, unique = TRUE)
RawCounts <- as.matrix(RawCounts[, -1])

SampleTable$condition <- ifelse(grepl("^brown", SampleTable$fat_type, ignore.case = TRUE), "BAT", "WAT")
SampleTable <- SampleTable[, c("run_accession", "sample_alias", "sample_number", "fat_type", "condition")]
row.names(SampleTable) <- SampleTable$sample_alias

SampleTable <- SampleTable[colnames(RawCounts), , drop = FALSE]
SampleTable$condition <- factor(SampleTable$condition, levels = c("WAT", "BAT"))

dds_batlas <- DESeqDataSetFromMatrix(
  countData = RawCounts,
  colData = SampleTable,
  design = ~ condition)

dds_batlas
```

It is good practice to inspect the library sizes and remove genes with zero counts across all samples before normalization.

```{r, eval=TRUE}
colSums(counts(dds_batlas)) %>% barplot(
  las = 2,
  main = "Library sizes - Total reads assigned to genes")
```

```{r, eval=TRUE}
keep_genes <- rowSums(counts(dds_batlas)) > 0 # keep only those that have expression
dds_batlas <- dds_batlas[keep_genes, ]

dim(dds_batlas)
```

DESeq2 size factors were estimatedto account for differences in sequencing depth and RNA composition across samples. This results in a more comparable normalized counts matrix.

```{r, eval=TRUE}
dds_batlas <- estimateSizeFactors(dds_batlas)

plot(
  sizeFactors(dds_batlas),
  colSums(counts(dds_batlas)),
  xlab = "Size factors",
  ylab = "Library sizes",
  cex = 0.8, # make points smaller for better visibility
  pch = 19) # solid points

counts_sf_normalized <- counts(dds_batlas, normalized = TRUE)
```

Normalized counts are more comparable across samples. This is reflected in the boxplots of the log2 counts before and after normalization, where the non-normalized counts show more variability in their distributions across samples, while the normalized counts show more similar distributions.

```{r, eval=TRUE, fig.width=10, fig.height=6}
par(mfrow = c(1, 2))

boxplot(
  log2(counts(dds_batlas) + 1),
  notch = TRUE,
  main = "Non-normalized read counts",
  ylab = "log2(read counts)",
  cex = 0.6,
  las = 2)

boxplot(
  log2(counts(dds_batlas, normalized = TRUE) + 1),
  notch = TRUE,
  main = "Size-factor-normalized read counts",
  ylab = "log2(read counts)",
  cex = 0.6,
  las = 2)
```

To stabilize the variance for exploratory analyses such as PCA (heterskedacity violate underlaying assumptions), data was `rlog` transformed.

```{r, eval=TRUE}
assay(dds_batlas, "log_counts") <- log2(counts(dds_batlas, normalized = FALSE) + 1)
assay(dds_batlas, "log_norm_counts") <- log2(counts(dds_batlas, normalized = TRUE) + 1)

dst_rlog <- rlog(dds_batlas, blind = TRUE)
rlog_norm_counts <- assay(dst_rlog)

par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
plot(
  assay(dds_batlas, "log_norm_counts")[, 1:2],
  cex = 0.1,
  main = "log2 normalized counts")

plot(
  assay(dst_rlog)[, 1:2],
  cex = 0.1,
  main = "rlog transformed",
  xlab = colnames(assay(dst_rlog[, 1])),
  ylab = colnames(assay(dst_rlog[, 2])))
```

Using the variance-stabilized `rlog` values, we can now run a PCA to visualize the main axes of variation across samples and assess whether BAT and WAT separate globally.

```{r, eval=TRUE}
pca_data <- plotPCA(dst_rlog, intgroup = "condition", returnData = TRUE, ntop=500)
percent_var <- round(100 * attr(pca_data, "percentVar"))

ggplot(pca_data, aes(PC1, PC2, color = condition, label = name)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(vjust = -0.8, size = 3) +
  xlab(paste0("PC1: ", percent_var[1], "% variance")) +
  ylab(paste0("PC2: ", percent_var[2], "% variance")) +
  ggtitle("PCA of rlog-transformed counts") +
  theme_bw()
```

PC1 shows a modest 35% variance, enough to separate most of the BAT samples from the WAT samples. One exception is `E387_7_brown_fat`, which clusters with WAT while being labeled as BAT. PC2 captures around 19% of the variance and shows some separation within the BAT samples, but does not clearly separate BAT from WAT.

PCA analysis reveals `E387_7_brown_fat` is relatively transcriptomically similar to the rest of the`WAT` samples. Since these human samples were taken surgically and Brown adipose tissue is difficult to discern from other tissues in adult humans, it is possible that this BAT sample is actually white adipose tissue. For this reason, I decided to exclude it from the transcriptomic quantification.

```{r, eval=TRUE}
dds_batlas <- DESeq(dds_batlas)
res_bat_vs_wat <- results(dds_batlas, contrast = c("condition", "BAT", "WAT"))
res_bat_vs_wat <- res_bat_vs_wat[order(res_bat_vs_wat$padj), ]
```

```{r, eval=TRUE}
summary(res_bat_vs_wat)
```

For redeability, `biomaRt` package was used to retrieve HGNC symbols and use those as row names in the volcano plot.

```{r BioMart, eval=TRUE}
library(biomaRt)

Vulcano <- as.data.frame(res_bat_vs_wat)
Vulcano$ensembl_gene_id <- sub("\\..*$", "", rownames(Vulcano)) # remove version tail

gene_map <- tryCatch({
  mart <- useEnsembl(biomart = "genes", dataset = "hsapiens_gene_ensembl")
  out <- getBM(
    attributes = c("ensembl_gene_id", "hgnc_symbol"),
    filters = "ensembl_gene_id",
    values = unique(Vulcano$ensembl_gene_id),
    mart = mart)
  out[out$hgnc_symbol != "", , drop = FALSE]
}, error = function(e) {
  message("biomaRt unavailable; using Ensembl IDs as plot labels. ", conditionMessage(e))
  data.frame(
    ensembl_gene_id = character(),
    hgnc_symbol = character(),
    stringsAsFactors = FALSE
  )
})

Vulcano <- merge(Vulcano, gene_map, by = "ensembl_gene_id", all.x = TRUE, sort = FALSE)
Vulcano$label <- ifelse(
  is.na(Vulcano$hgnc_symbol) | Vulcano$hgnc_symbol == "",
  rownames(as.data.frame(res_bat_vs_wat)),
  Vulcano$hgnc_symbol
)
Vulcano <- Vulcano[match(sub("\\..*$", "", rownames(as.data.frame(res_bat_vs_wat))), Vulcano$ensembl_gene_id), ]
Vulcano$Gene <- rownames(as.data.frame(res_bat_vs_wat))
Vulcano <- Vulcano[, !(names(Vulcano) %in% c("Gene", "hgnc_symbol", "ensembl_gene_id"))]
```

```{r, eval=TRUE}
knitr::kable(head(Vulcano, 15), caption = "Top 15 genes in the Vulcano table after BioMart annotation")
```

#### Volcano plot of BAT vs WAT differential expression results

```{r, eval=TRUE ,fig.width=10, fig.height=8}
library(EnhancedVolcano)

EnhancedVolcano(
  Vulcano,
  lab = Vulcano$label,
  x = "log2FoldChange",
  y = "padj",
  pCutoff = 0.05,
  FCcutoff = 0.5,
  title = "BAT vs WAT differential expression",
  subtitle = "DESeq2 results",
  caption = "Adjusted P = BH FDR",
  col = c("grey80", "steelblue3", "goldenrod2", "coral"))
```

Differential expression analysis identified many genes that are significantly differentially expressed between BAT and WAT, with some of the most significant ones being *`UCP1`*, *`CIDEA`*, and *`PPARGC1A`*, which are known markers of brown adipose tissue. This suggests that our analysis is capturing biologically meaningful differences between the two tissue types, and validates the BATLAS dataset as a useful resource for studying the transcriptomic characteristics of brown and white adipose tissue in humans.

Next, I will take the BAT samples (except for the `E387_7_brown_fat` sample that clusters with WAT) and merge them with the GTEx data to see how they compare globally.

### GSEA analysis of a custom brown fat signature

Gene Set Enrichment Analysis (GSEA) was performed using a custom brown fat signature to confirm that the BAT samples are indeed enriched for brown adipose tissue markers compared to WAT.

```{r GSEA, eval=TRUE}
library(fgsea)

brown_fat_signature <- c(
  "UCP1", "CIDEA", "DIO2", "PRDM16", "PPARGC1A", "EBF2", "ZIC1", "LHX8",
  "ELOVL3", "CKMT1", "FABP3", "COX7A1", "SLC27A2", "KCNK3", "COBL", "HMGCS2",
  "TGM2", "NRG4", "BMP8B", "GPR120", "SLC29A1", "HOXA2", "ADCY3", "GYK",
  "PREX1", "CTTNBP2", "ACTC1", "SSTR1", "DMRTA1", "EDNRB"
)
brown_fat_signature <- unique(brown_fat_signature)

rank_tbl <- as.data.frame(res_bat_vs_wat) %>%
  tibble::rownames_to_column("gene_id_version") %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    ensembl_gene_id = sub("\\..*$", "", gene_id_version),
    hgnc_symbol = gene_map$hgnc_symbol[
      match(sub("\\..*$", "", gene_id_version), gene_map$ensembl_gene_id)]) %>%
  dplyr::filter(is.finite(stat), !is.na(hgnc_symbol), hgnc_symbol != "") %>%
  dplyr::group_by(hgnc_symbol) %>%
  dplyr::slice_max(order_by = abs(stat), n = 1, with_ties = FALSE) %>%
  dplyr::ungroup() %>%
  dplyr::arrange(desc(stat))

ranks <- rank_tbl$stat
names(ranks) <- rank_tbl$hgnc_symbol

overlap_genes <- intersect(names(ranks), brown_fat_signature)
message("Ranked genes used for GSEA: ", length(ranks))
message("Genes in custom brown fat signature: ", length(unique(brown_fat_signature)))
message("Overlapping genes tested by GSEA: ", length(overlap_genes))

gsea_res <- fgsea::fgseaMultilevel(
  pathways = list(SIGNATURE_BROWN_FAT = brown_fat_signature),
  stats = ranks,
  minSize = 5,
  maxSize = 500
) %>%
  dplyr::mutate(
    leading_edge = vapply(leadingEdge, function(x) paste(unique(x), collapse = ", "), character(1))
  )

knitr::kable(
  gsea_res %>%
    dplyr::select(pathway, size, ES, NES, pval, padj, leading_edge),
  digits = 3,
  caption = "GSEA of a custom BATLAS/ProFAT brown fat signature in BAT vs WAT"
)

fgsea::plotEnrichment(brown_fat_signature, ranks) +
  labs(
    title = "BAT vs WAT GSEA",
    subtitle = "SIGNATURE_BROWN_FAT (BATLAS/ProFAT markers)",
    x = "Genes ranked by DESeq2 Wald statistic",
    y = "Running enrichment score") +
  theme_bw()
```

### Merging BAT samples with GTEx data

#### Generating BAT TPM values with RSEM and STAR

To merge the BATLAS samples with GTEx data, the same GENCODE v39 annotation that GTEx used for their v10 release was used to maximize comparability.

GTEx TPM values for other non-BAT tissues can be found [here](https://www.gtexportal.org/home/downloads/adult-gtex/metadata)

```{bash}
# same annotation as the one GTEX where using for v10
wget https://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/gencode/Gencode_human/release_39/gencode.v39.annotation.gtf.gz
gunzip -c gencode.v39.annotation.gtf.gz > gencode.v39.annotation.gtf

wget https://ftp.ebi.ac.uk/pub/databases/gencode/Gencode_human/release_39/GRCh38.primary_assembly.genome.fa.gz
gunzip GRCh38.primary_assembly.genome.fa.gz

# IMPORTANT! The FASTA file was downloaded from GTEX web page directly.
```

GTEx uses RSEM 1.3.3 for quantification, and this analysis uses that same version to maximize comparability.

```{bash}
# Create a conda environment for RSEM and its dependencies
mamba create -n RSEM -y \
  star=2.7.10a \
  samtools \
  gcc \
  gxx \
  make \
  zlib \
  perl
```

```{bash}
wget https://github.com/deweylab/RSEM/archive/refs/tags/v1.3.3.tar.gz -O rsem-1.3.3.tar.gz
tar -xzf rsem-1.3.3.tar.gz
cd RSEM-1.3.3

make # to compile RSEM

echo 'export PATH=/storage/scratch01/groups/md/microproteins/BROWN_GTEx_TPM_STAR/Cohort_2/tools/RSEM-1.3.3:$PATH' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc
```

Building the STAR index for RSEM:

`sbatch run_rsem_star_all.sbatch`
```{bash}
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=rsem_star_ref
#SBATCH --output=rsem_star_ref_%j.out
#SBATCH --error=rsem_star_ref_%j.err
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=64G

source ~/.bashrc
mamba activate RSEM

cd /storage/scratch01/groups/md/microproteins/BROWN_GTEx_TPM_STAR

rsem-prepare-reference \
  --gtf gencode.v39.annotation.gtf \
  --star \
  GRCh38.primary_assembly.genome.fa \
  rsem_star_reference/gencode_v39
```

RSEM was run on all samples except for `E387_7_brown_fat` that was removed because it clusters with WAT.

`run_rsem_star_all.sbatch`
```{bash}
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=rsem_star_all
#SBATCH --output=rsem_star_all_%j.out
#SBATCH --error=rsem_star_all_%j.err
#SBATCH --time=24:00:00
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task=8
#SBATCH --mem=48G

source ~/.bashrc
mamba activate RSEM

cd /storage/scratch01/groups/md/microproteins/BROWN_GTEx_TPM_STAR/Cohort_2

mkdir -p rsem_results

REF_PREFIX="/storage/scratch01/groups/md/microproteins/BROWN_GTEx_TPM_STAR/Cohort_2/rsem_star_reference/gencode_v39"
STAR_PATH=$(dirname "$(which STAR)")

for fastq in ERR*.fastq.gz; do
    sample=$(basename "$fastq" .fastq.gz)
    echo "Quantifying sample: $sample"

    rsem-calculate-expression \
        --star \
        --star-path "$STAR_PATH" \
        --star-gzipped-read-file \
        -p 8 \
        "$fastq" \
        "$REF_PREFIX" \
        "rsem_results/${sample}"

    echo "Done: $sample"
    echo "-----------------------------------------"
done
```

#### Merging BATLAS RSEM TPM values with GTEx TPM values

To get a more comparable TPM matrix between the BATLAS samples and the GTEx data, the average TPM across the 9 BAT samples was computed to get a single "BAT" column.

`compute_average_tpm.R`
```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(readr)
library(purrr)

# All gene-level result files
gene_files <- list.files(pattern = "\\.genes\\.results$")

# Read each file: gene_id + TPM, TPM column named by sample
read_one <- function(f) {
  sample <- sub("\\.genes\\.results$", "", f)
  read_tsv(f, show_col_types = FALSE) %>%
    select(gene_id, TPM) %>%
    rename(!!sample := TPM)}

gene_list <- lapply(gene_files, read_one)

# Merge on gene_id
merged_tpm <- reduce(gene_list, full_join, by = "gene_id")

# Replace NAs with 0
merged_tpm[is.na(merged_tpm)] <- 0

# Row-wise mean TPM
avg_tpm <- merged_tpm %>%
  rowwise() %>%
  mutate(mean_TPM = mean(c_across(-gene_id))) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  select(gene_id, mean_TPM)

# Save
write_tsv(avg_tpm, "average_TPM_across_samples_cohort_2.tsv")
```

GTEX transcript abundance data (`GTEx_Analysis_2022-06-06_v10_RNASeQCv2.4.2_gene_median_tpm.gct`) was merged with the BATLAS average TPM values to get a combined matrix of TPM values across all tissues.

```{r nTPM_matrix, eval=TRUE}
data <- read_tsv("GTEx_Analysis_2022-06-06_v10_RNASeQCv2.4.2_gene_median_tpm.gct")
data <- data %>%
  mutate(Gene = sub("\\..*$", "", Gene))  # remove .version in ensembl id

bat_tpm <- read_tsv("average_TPM_across_samples_cohort_2.tsv") # quantificatoin using RSEM, same process GTEx used
bat_tpm_value_col <- intersect(c("BAT_TPM", "mean_TPM"), names(bat_tpm)) # I have to versions...

bat_tpm <- bat_tpm %>%
  mutate(
    Gene = sub("\\..*$", "", gene_id),  # remove .version in ensembl id
    Adipose_Brown = .data[[bat_tpm_value_col]]
  ) %>%
  dplyr::select(Gene, Adipose_Brown)

# annotate gene types
mart <- useEnsembl("ensembl", dataset = "hsapiens_gene_ensembl")

gene_types <- getBM(
    attributes = c("ensembl_gene_id", "gene_biotype"),
    filters = "ensembl_gene_id",
    values = unique(data$Gene),
    mart = mart)

data_pc <- if ("gene_biotype" %in% colnames(gene_types)) {
  data %>%
    inner_join(gene_types, by = c("Gene" = "ensembl_gene_id")) %>%
    filter(gene_biotype == "protein_coding")
} else {
  data
}

merged_tpm <- data_pc %>%
  left_join(bat_tpm, by = "Gene", relationship = "many-to-many") # merge the GTEx TPM with the BAT TPM using the gene id as key. This is a many-to-many relationship because some genes have multiple transcripts, and thus multiple rows in the GTEx data, but only one row in the BAT TPM data (since RSEM quantifies at the gene level by summing across transcripts)

merged_tpm$Adipose_Brown <- as.numeric(merged_tpm$Adipose_Brown) # this gives us a "raw" TPM matrix

# GTEx does not provide normalized TPMs (nTPMs), but HPA does a two step normalization using the following approach:
# They do between-sample normalization using TMM (edgeR-style) across all samples within a dataset.

# Build and unnormalized TPM matrix
tissue_cols <- setdiff(colnames(merged_tpm), c("Gene", "Gene.name"))

tpm_mat <- merged_tpm %>%
  dplyr::select(-any_of(c("gene_biotype", "Gene", "Gene.name"))) %>%  # remove annotation cols if present; keep only tissues
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric)) %>%  # make columns numeric
  as.matrix() # turn into a matrix

rownames(tpm_mat) <- merged_tpm$Gene.name # or Gene

# Total TPM per tissue (column)
col_sums <- colSums(tpm_mat)

# pTPM = TPM / total_TPM_for_that_tissue * 1e6
pTPM <- sweep(tpm_mat, 2, col_sums, FUN = "/") * 1e6

library(edgeR) # edger for TMM normalization

# Treat pTPM as pseudo-counts in a DGEList
dge <- DGEList(counts = pTPM)

# Compute TMM normalization factors
dge <- calcNormFactors(dge, method = "TMM")

# Effective library sizes after TMM (edgeR always uses original lib.size * norm.factor)
eff_libsizes <- colSums(pTPM) * dge$samples$norm.factors

# nTPM = TMM-normalized pTPM, scaled back to per-million
nTPM_mat <- t( t(pTPM) / eff_libsizes ) * 1e6

nTPM_df <- nTPM_mat %>%
  as.data.frame() %>%
  rownames_to_column("Gene") %>%
  left_join(merged_tpm %>% dplyr::select(Gene), by = "Gene")

# Optional: reorder columns for convenience
nTPM_df <- nTPM_df %>%
  relocate(Gene)

write_csv(nTPM_df, "nTPM_df.csv")
```

#### UMAP of the merged dataset

```{r UMAP, eval=TRUE}
# UMAP transcript abundance

library(uwot)
library(RColorBrewer)

nTPM_df <- read_csv("nTPM_df.csv")

# Build matrix (genes x tissues)
stopifnot(ncol(nTPM_df) >= 3)
stopifnot(colnames(nTPM_df)[1] %in% c("Symbol", "gene", "Gene", "Gene.name"))

nTPM_df <- nTPM_df %>%
  rename(Symbol = 1)

tissue_cols <- setdiff(colnames(nTPM_df), "Symbol")

expr_mat <- nTPM_df %>%
  dplyr::select(all_of(tissue_cols)) %>%
  mutate(across(everything(), as.numeric)) %>%
  as.matrix()

expr_mat_z <- t(scale(t(expr_mat))) # row-wise z-score

rownames(expr_mat_z) <- nTPM_df$Symbol

# BUT for building the signature matrix, we use expr_mat_z

ENRICHMENT_THRESHOLD <- 1 

# Using this "threshold" means there is no thresshold, and most genes will be included which is what we want in this case
# This is here in case I want to define a more stringent threshold for what counts as "enriched" in a tissue. For example,
# HPA uses a 4-fold threshold, meaning the max expression in the dominant tissue must be at least 4 times higher than the 
# second highest expression across tissues for that gene to be considered a "signature gene"

max1 <- apply(expr_mat, 1, max, na.rm = TRUE) # get the max value of each row
dominant_tissue <- tissue_cols[max.col(expr_mat, ties.method = "first")] # get the colname for each max value

second_max <- apply(expr_mat, 1, function(x) {
  x <- x[is.finite(x)]
  x_sorted <- sort(x, decreasing = TRUE) # sort them decreasing
  x_sorted[2]}) # get the second one

is_signature <- is.finite(max1) & (max1 > 0) & is.finite(second_max) &
  ((second_max == 0 & max1 > 0) | (max1 >= ENRICHMENT_THRESHOLD * second_max))

# Here we use the z-scored matrix
sig_genes <- rownames(expr_mat_z)[is_signature]
sig_mat   <- expr_mat_z[sig_genes, , drop = FALSE] # use the z-score matrix

message("Signature genes used for background UMAP: ", length(sig_genes))

# Fit UMAP on signature genes; keep model for transform. We will use seed = 3.
bg_umap <- uwot::umap(
  sig_mat,
  n_neighbors = 20, # 20 is a common default, but can be tuned based on the dataset size and expected local structure
  min_dist    = 0.1, # 0.1 is a common default, but can be tuned to control how tightly UMAP packs points together
  repulsion_strength = 1, # 
  metric      = "correlation", # pearson correlation distance. This is for better visualization
  n_components = 2,
  ret_model = TRUE,
  verbose = TRUE,
  seed = 5)

# With ret_model=TRUE, bg_umap is a LIST; coords are in $embedding
stopifnot(is.list(bg_umap), !is.null(bg_umap$embedding))

bg_emb <- as.matrix(bg_umap$embedding)
stopifnot(ncol(bg_emb) == 2)

bg_df <- tibble(
  Symbol = sig_genes,
  Dominant.tissue = dominant_tissue[match(sig_genes, rownames(expr_mat_z))],
  UMAP1 = bg_emb[, 1],
  UMAP2 = bg_emb[, 2])


# Tissue grouping based on GTEx sample annotations and manual curation. This is used for coloring the UMAP plot and for building the signature matrix later on. 
# The grouping is somewhat subjective, but it is based on known biological relationships between tissues and their expected expression profiles.
cns_labels <- c(
  "Brain_Amygdala",
  "Brain_Anterior_cingulate_cortex_BA24",
  "Brain_Caudate_basal_ganglia",
  "Brain_Cerebellar_Hemisphere",
  "Brain_Cerebellum",
  "Brain_Cortex",
  "Brain_Frontal_Cortex_BA9",
  "Brain_Hippocampus",
  "Brain_Hypothalamus",
  "Brain_Nucleus_accumbens_basal_ganglia",
  "Brain_Putamen_basal_ganglia",
  "Brain_Spinal_cord_cervical_c-1",
  "Brain_Substantia_nigra"
)

colon <- c(
  "Colon_Sigmoid",
  "Colon_Transverse",
  "Colon_Transverse_Mixed_Cell",
  "Colon_Transverse_Mucosa",
  "Colon_Transverse_Muscularis"
)

liver <- c(
  "Liver",
  "Liver_Hepatocyte",
  "Liver_Mixed_Cell",
  "Liver_Portal_Tract"
)

esophagus <- c(
  "Esophagus_Gastroesophageal_Junction",
  "Esophagus_Mucosa",
  "Esophagus_Muscularis"
)

heart <- c(
  "Heart_Atrial_Appendage",
  "Heart_Left_Ventricle"
)

kidney <- c(
  "Kidney_Cortex",
  "Kidney_Medulla"
)

stomach <- c(
  "Stomach_Mixed_Cell",
  "Stomach_Mucosa",
  "Stomach_Muscularis"
)

female_sex_labels <- c(
  "Cervix_Ectocervix",
  "Cervix_Endocervix",
  "Ovary",
  "Vagina",
  "Uterus",
  "Fallopian_Tube"
)

male_sex_labels <- c(
  "Testis",
  "Prostate"
)

sex_labels <- c(
  "Testis",
  "Prostate",
  "Cervix_Ectocervix",
  "Cervix_Endocervix",
  "Ovary",
  "Vagina",
  "Uterus",
  "Fallopian_Tube"
)

small_intestine <- c(
  "Small_Intestine_Terminal_Ileum",
  "Small_Intestine_Terminal_Ileum_Lymphode_Aggregate",
  "Small_Intestine_Terminal_Ileum_Mixed_Cell"
)

pancreas <- c(
  "Pancreas",
  "Pancreas_Acini",
  "Pancreas_Islets",
  "Pancreas_Mixed_Cell"
)

arteries <- c(
  "Artery_Aorta",
  "Artery_Tibial",
  "Artery_Coronary"
)
skin <- c(
  "Skin_Not_Sun_Exposed_Suprapubic",
  "Skin_Sun_Exposed_Lower_leg"
)

fibroblasts  <- c("Cells_Cultured_fibroblasts")
limphocytes  <- c("Cells_EBV-transformed_lymphocytes")
tibial_nerve <- c("Nerve_Tibial")
whole_blood  <- c("Whole_Blood")

pool_tissue_group <- function(dominant) {
  dplyr::case_when(
    dominant %in% cns_labels ~ "CNS regions",
    
    dominant %in% female_sex_labels ~ "female sex organs",
    dominant %in% male_sex_labels ~ "male sex organs",
    # dominant %in% sex_labels ~ "sex organs",
    
    dominant %in% fibroblasts  ~ "fibroblasts",
    dominant %in% limphocytes  ~ "limphocytes",
    dominant %in% tibial_nerve ~ "tibial nerve",
    dominant %in% whole_blood  ~ "whole blood",
    
    dominant %in% arteries ~ "arteries",
    dominant %in% liver    ~ "liver",
    dominant %in% kidney   ~ "kidney",
    dominant %in% stomach  ~ "stomach",
    dominant == "Stomach"  ~ "stomach",
    dominant %in% esophagus ~ "esophagus",
    dominant %in% colon     ~ "colon",
    dominant %in% heart     ~ "heart muscle",
    
    dominant == "Adipose_Subcutaneous"     ~ "adipose tissue subcutaneous",
    dominant == "Adipose_Visceral_Omentum" ~ "adipose tissue omentum",
    dominant == "Adipose_Brown"            ~ "adipose tissue brown",
    
    dominant == "Lung" ~ "lung",
    dominant == "Adrenal_Gland" ~ "adrenal gland",
    dominant == "Muscle_Skeletal" ~ "skeletal muscle",
    dominant %in% skin ~ "skin",
    dominant == "Thyroid" ~ "thyroid gland",
    dominant == "Minor_Salivary_Gland" ~ "salivary gland",
    dominant == "Spleen" ~ "spleen",
    dominant == "Bladder" ~ "bladder",
    dominant %in% small_intestine ~ "small intestine",
    dominant %in% pancreas ~ "pancreas",
    dominant == "Pituitary" ~ "pituitary gland",
    dominant == "Breast_Mammary_Tissue" ~ "breast mammary tissue",
    
    TRUE ~ dominant
  )
}

bg_df <- bg_df %>% mutate(Tissue_group = pool_tissue_group(Dominant.tissue))

# Colors (same palette logic as the script)
tissue_colors <- c(
  "breast mammary tissue" = "#ff69b4",
  "fibroblasts"      = "#708bf5",
  "limphocytes"      = "#eeb5e4",
  "arteries"         = "#f58d89",
  "liver"            = "#ff0505",
  "kidney"           = "#ed2128",
  "CNS regions"      = "#dfb7f1",
  "female sex organs" = "#bffaff",
  "male sex organs"  = "#8ffabb",
  "sex organs"       = "#037a32",
  "stomach"          = "#6BAED6",
  "esophagus"        = "#edf02d",
  "retina"           = "#367846",
  "colon"            = "#fc51db",
  "adrenal gland"    = "#8bc4af",
  "skeletal muscle"  = "#6e6785",
  "spinal cord"      = "#9E9AC8",
  "vWAT"             = "#fcbf68",
  "scWAT"            = "#ebcaa0",
  "BAT    "          = "#26ff00",
  "spleen"           = "#07a88b",
  "lung"             = "#90CAF9",
  "thyroid gland"    = "#F091F8",
  "salivary gland"   = "#B39DDB",
  "heart muscle"     = "#3b0cf7",
  "pituitary gland"  = "#603bf5",
  "pancreas"         = "#3f558c",
  "skin"             = "#626e35",
  "small intestine"  = "#6a74e6",
  "arteries"         = "#f58d89",
  "tibial nerve"     = "#34daf8",
  "whole blood"      = "#f2555a",
  "bladder"          = "#f4a261")

all_groups   <- sort(unique(bg_df$Tissue_group))
extra_groups <- setdiff(all_groups, names(tissue_colors))
extra_cols <- setNames(
  colorRampPalette(brewer.pal(8, "Dark2"))(max(1, length(extra_groups))),
  extra_groups)

tissue_colors_full <- c(tissue_colors, extra_cols)
```

```{r UMAP_plot, eval=TRUE, fig.width=20, fig.height=10}
# Plot
BG_ALPHA <- 0.05
BG_SIZE  <- 5

p <- ggplot() +
  geom_point(
    data = bg_df,
    aes(x = UMAP1, y = UMAP2, color = Tissue_group),
    alpha = BG_ALPHA, size = BG_SIZE,
    show.legend = TRUE
) +
  scale_color_manual(values = tissue_colors_full, name = "Tissue group") +
  guides(color = guide_legend(override.aes = list(alpha = 1))) +
  theme_bw(base_family = "Arial", base_size = 14) +
  theme(
    plot.title   = element_text(size = 18),
    axis.title   = element_text(size = 16),
    axis.text    = element_text(size = 14),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 14),
    legend.text  = element_text(size = 12)
    # panel.grid = element_blank(),
    # legend.position = "right"
) +
  labs(
    title = "Background tissue-signature UMAP",
    # subtitle = paste0(
    #   "Signature rule: top >= ", ENRICHMENT_THRESHOLD, "x second; ",
    #   "UMAP: n_neighbors=", N_NEIGHBORS,
    #   ", min_dist=", MIN_DIST,
    #   ", metric=", METRIC),
    x = "UMAP1", y = "UMAP2")

print(p)
```

```{r, eval=TRUE}
sessionInfo()
```

# Results

BAT samples show more mitocondrial genes on average, which is expected given the high mitochondrial content of brown adipose tissue. PCA analysis reveals that most BAT samples cluster together and are distinct from WAT samples, except for one BAT sample (`E387_7_brown_fat`) that clusters with WAT. This suggests that `E387_7_brown_fat` may be misannotated or contaminated with WAT, and it was excluded from further analysis.

BAT samples show a clear enrichment for known brown adipose tissue markers such as *`UCP1`*, *`CIDEA`*, and *`PPARGC1A`* compared to WAT, which validates the BATLAS dataset as a useful resource for studying brown vs white adipose tissue transcriptomics in humans.

GSEA analysis using a custom brown fat signature derived from BATLAS/ProFAT markers confirms that the BAT samples are significantly enriched for brown adipose tissue markers compared to WAT, further supporting the biological relevance of the differential expression results.

When the BAT samples are merged with GTEx data and visualized using UMAP based on a background of tissue-specific signature genes, the BAT samples cluster separately from most other tissues, including WAT, and show some proximity to other metabolically active tissues such as skeletal muscle and liver. This suggests that the transcriptomic profile of BAT is distinct from WAT and shares some similarities with other metabolically active tissues.


# Discussion
